name: Californium
name_origin: Named after the U.S. state of California and the University of California, where it was first synthesized.

discovery.discoverer: Stanley G. Thompson, Kenneth Street Jr., Albert Ghiorso, Glenn T. Seaborg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in measurable amounts in nature; produced by intense neutron irradiation of curium and berkelium targets at high-flux research reactors.

compounds.name: Californium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Yellow-green solid; the most stable californium oxide, used in fundamental chemistry studies of the heavy actinides.

compounds.name: Californium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Green crystalline solid that serves as a typical starting point for californium solution and coordination chemistry.

compounds.name: Californium oxychloride
compounds.summary: Solid intermediate produced during fluoride/oxide processing of californium isotopes.

uses: Compact, intense neutron sources for reactor startup, oil-well logging, and detection of explosives and gold/silver ores (Cf-252)
Neutron activation analysis of trace elements
Target material for synthesizing heavier elements including oganesson (element 118)
Identification of metal corrosion and material defects via portable neutron radiography
reactivity_summary: A silvery, soft metal that can be cut with a knife. It reacts with steam, air, and acids. Like other heavy actinides, the +3 state dominates its chemistry, although +2 is more accessible than in earlier actinides. Californium-252 is intensely radioactive and a powerful spontaneous-fission neutron emitter.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Californium-251 has the longest half-life (898 years). The most important isotope by far is Cf-252 (half-life 2.645 years), a strong spontaneous-fission neutron source emitting about 2.3 × 10^12 neutrons per gram per second.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
